---
title: "Chapter 2: Regional MPM pipelines"
subtitle: "Mineral prospectivity on public open data: four regions, four deposit types"
sidebar: regional
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## What this chapter is about

This chapter walks through four regional pipelines I built: Eastern
Alaska (porphyry Cu-Mo), the BC Golden Triangle (multi-deposit), the
California Mother Lode (orogenic Au), and the Arizona porphyry-Cu
belt. The stack is the same in each one: USGS, NRCan, and
state-survey open data; feature-frame assembly with
`build_feature_frame()`; Random Forest classification with 20-km
spatial-block cross-validation; leak-free out-of-fold scoring; and
bootstrap confidence intervals on AUC and top-k% capture rates. Each
region is its own geologically distinct problem and tests whether
the approach holds up.

![Tanacross (eastern Alaska) porphyry-Cu prospectivity: random forest output across the bounded study area. Yellow cells are the highest-ranked; red crosses are the 56 ARDF porphyry positives held out for evaluation.](./data/derived/eastak/fig_prospectivity_tanacross.png)

## Headline results

| Region | Commodity | Positives | Top-1% capture | Top-5% capture | Spatial-block CV AUC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Eastern Alaska v1 (Tanacross + Mt Hayes + Nabesna) | porphyry Cu-Mo | 45 | 22.2% (22×) | 64% (13×) | n/a |
| BC Golden Triangle v2 (multi-deposit) | Cu / Au / Ag / Zn | 154 (blind set) | n/a | n/a | n/a |
| Mother Lode v3 / v3.1 (Sierra foothills) | orogenic Au | 6,149 | n/a | 19.5% (4×) | 0.85 [0.79, 0.90] |
| Arizona v4 (SE porphyry belt) | porphyry Cu | 191 | 23.0% (23×) | 51.3% (10×) | n/a |

Top-1% and top-5% are capture rates: fraction of held-out positives
in the top k% of cells the model ranks most prospective. The
parenthetical ×N is the enrichment factor over what a random sample
would produce. The 22× enrichment on Tanacross and the 23×
enrichment on Arizona sit in the same regime across two porphyry-Cu
provinces 4,000 km apart. That's the central reproducibility claim
the four-region work supports.

## How the pipeline works

Federal and state agencies publish decades of geophysical,
geochemical, and geological data on national grids. My pipeline:

1. **Fetch** raw rasters and shapefiles from public APIs: USGS
   aeromagnetic and Bouguer/isostatic gravity, USGS State Geologic
   Map Compilation (SGMC) lithology and structural faults, USGS
   Mineral Resources Data System (MRDS) occurrence records, USGS
   National Geochemical Database (NGDB) stream-sediment chemistry,
   USGS 30-m DEM, Sentinel-2 SWIR alteration indices, plus
   region-specific feeds (Alaska Geochemical Database AGDB4 v4 for
   Tanacross; British Columbia Geological Survey RGS/MINFILE for
   BCGT).
2. **Reproject** every layer onto a common equal-area CRS
   (California Albers EPSG:3310 for the CONUS work; NAD83/Alaska
   Albers for Tanacross). Resample onto a 500-m grid.
3. **Assemble** the per-cell feature vector: geophysics columns,
   four magnetic-field derivatives (1VD, HGM, analytic signal,
   tilt), pathfinder geochemistry by element (5-km radius mean,
   max, count, has-data flag), SGMC top-level lithology and
   MAJOR1/2/3 fine-grained lithology, distance-to-fault, Sentinel-2
   indices, slope, aspect, and terrain ruggedness from the DEM.
4. **Clean labels** via Cox-Singer-aware filtering of the MRDS
   free-text deposit-type field. For Mother Lode this drops placer,
   epithermal, and skarn-Au records and keeps in-place orogenic-Au;
   for Tanacross it keeps porphyry-Cu plus distal-porphyry; for
   Arizona it keeps porphyry-Cu while excluding porphyry-Mo and
   skarn-Cu (tracked for diagnostics).
5. **Train** Random Forest with `class_weight='balanced_subsample'`,
   pseudo-negatives sampled with a 5-km exclusion zone around any
   known occurrence.
6. **Score** with 20-km spatial-block CV. Every cell is predicted
   by a model that did not train on its block. Bootstrap CIs on AUC
   and top-k% capture.

## Eastern Alaska: three channels of exploration bias

The Eastern Alaska sensitivity pass made me look hard at how a
pseudo-supervised pipeline lets exploration history leak into the
features. The bias arrives through three channels, even after the
obvious `*_count_5km` columns get dropped. First, count-level: explored
cells just have more samples, and the count features expose that
directly. Second, NaN-versus-value: cells with zero samples become
sentinel-filled in the feature matrix, so the random forest can split
on "sentinel vs real value" as a proxy for "explored vs unexplored,"
though the `*_has_data` indicator columns I added landed outside the
top-15 importances, so this channel is minor in practice. Third,
magnitude-within-explored: among already-sampled cells, the max of 100
samples runs mechanically larger than the max of 5, so `*_max_5km`
correlates with sampling density even on non-NaN rows.

## BCGT: the 366-collar blind test inversion

The BC Golden Triangle gave me a clean external test the other
regions could not. BCGS GeoFile 2025-11 publishes 366 post-2015 drill
collars in the AOI with per-hole element maxima. After restricting
to cells the training labels know nothing about, the blind subset is
241 cells: 154 drilled-positive and 87 drilled-negative. That lets me
build distributions of model probability at drilled-positive versus
drilled-negative cells on a truly held-out set. The result inverts
the expected direction. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test rejects equality
of the two distributions, but the Mann-Whitney one-sided test
(positives greater than negatives) fails. At the top-5% cutoff the
model flags 63% of drilled-negative cells against 40% of
drilled-positive cells, so the negatives get flagged 1.57x as often
as the positives. Both populations sit in the same already-explored
terrain near training positives, so they look equally prospective to
a model that learned exploration history as much as it learned ore
geology.

## Going wider on BCGT: dh2loop and the Loop3D bridge

The 366 post-2015 collars above are a slice of a much larger
dataset. The full BCGS Assessment Report Drillhole Database (GeoFile
2025-11) contains 6,460 collars going back to 1985, with 107k
lithology intervals and 5.5M long-format geochemical assays across
121k samples. That makes it the public Canadian analog of the
Western Australia WAMEX archive that KoBold's TerraShed ingests at
industrial scale.

To use this data for anything beyond the per-element maxima feature
table the prospectivity model already consumes, the operator-format
records need a translation step. dh2loop 1.0 (Joshi et al. 2021,
[GMD 14:6711](https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-14-6711-2021)) is the
Loop3D consortium's drillhole adapter: it produces standardized
Collar, Survey, and Lithology tables that LoopStructural, map2loop,
and the wider Loop3D 3D-modeling stack expect as input. I wrote
`scripts/bcgs_to_dh2loop.py` to ingest the BCGS relational
GeoPackage, reproject collars from five source UTM zones to a common
BC Albers grid (EPSG:3005), and write the four dh2loop-format
tables.

![BCGS drillholes converted to dh2loop format and rendered for the BCGT AOI. Left: 1,225 collars with their elevations, showing the topographic relief the dataset spans. Right: bedrock-top elevation surface, interpolated from per-hole contact depths inferred from the lithology table.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_bcgs_bedrock_plate.png)

Two findings from the conversion are worth being explicit about.

First, BCGS lithology entries are operator-format text, and dirty.
The 107,326 intervals are logged under 2,024 distinct strings.
Operators use seven distinct codes for overburden alone (`OVB`,
`OVBD`, `OB`, `OVER`, `Overburden`, plus mixed-case variants), four
for drill casing (`WCAS`, `CAS`, `CASN`, `CASE`), and a long tail of
acronyms (`TFML`, `AXXX`, `MNZD`, `KMZ`, `AnLAT`, `PMFP`) whose
meaning depends on which contractor logged the hole.

The dh2loop 757-term thesaurus exists precisely to normalize this
vocabulary. I applied it in two stages: a hand-built BCGS-specific
decoder for the operator shorthand we can confidently expand
(`OVB` → overburden, `MNZD` → monzodiorite, `DDRT` → diorite),
followed by the dh2loop hierarchical thesaurus via rapidfuzz
fuzzy-matching for the plain-English geological terms. The combined
pipeline standardizes **66.6% of the 107,326 intervals** (71,499
rows) into the dh2loop `Detailed_Lithology / Lithology_Subgroup /
Lithology_Group` triple. The remaining 33% is opaque operator-specific
shorthand whose decoding needs per-contractor decoder keys that
neither BCGS nor dh2loop ships. The normalized table is at
`data/derived/bcgs_dh2loop/Lithology_normalized.csv`; the script
is `scripts/bcgs_thesaurus_pass.py`. A discovery from this iteration:
dh2loop's `thesaurus_geology_lithology_code.csv` is WAMEX-specific
(per-company code lookup from Western Australian records); only one
of the 27 BCGS-AOI top-25 codes matches it directly. The hierarchical
thesaurus is portable because it matches on plain English; the
per-company code lookup is not.

Second, the LoopStructural piece needs more than dh2loop tables.
LoopStructural's implicit-foliation interpolator wants authentic
structural orientation observations (strike / dip vectors at known
locations), which a dh2loop Lithology table doesn't carry. The BCGS
GeoFile doesn't ship downhole-deviation surveys or per-interval
structural attitudes either. I tried fitting the bedrock-top surface
with LoopStructural by giving it synthetic horizontal orientations
at each contact point, which is mathematically incoherent: the
foliation degenerated to a near-constant z-value around 8 m above
sea level, ignoring the actual contact elevations (which span 526
to 1655 m across the AOI). The figure above uses scipy's
thin-plate-spline RBF interpolator instead, which is the appropriate
tool for the "estimate z from scattered (x, y, z) observations
without orientation data" problem this dataset actually presents.
LoopStructural becomes the right tool when the next iteration on
this thread adds correlated-prior and multi-hypothesis machinery
(D.6.B in the project's long-term plan).

The forward-looking value of the converted dataset is the pre/post-2010
collar split it enables. Inside the BCGT AOI, 230 collars were
drilled before 2010 and 744 in the 2010 to 2020 window.

![BCGS drillhole density inside the BCGT AOI, pre-2010 (left, n = 230) and 2010 to 2020 (right, n = 744). The exploration footprint shifted southeast over the decade, concentrating in what became the modern porphyry-epithermal corridor (the 140-collars-per-hex cluster is the KSM-Brucejack project area). This is the substrate for the D.6.B retrospective validation: the left pattern is what the v2.0 POMDP planner gets to train on; the right pattern is what it has to predict.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_bcgs_drill_density_split.png)

The substrate is now ready for a retrospective validation
experiment of the v2.0 POMDP planner from
[Chapter 7](drill_planning.qmd): train the prior on pre-2010
positives, ask the planner which cells to drill next, and compare
its recommendations against the cells operators actually drilled in
the 2010 to 2020 window. The dh2loop tables are at
`data/derived/bcgs_dh2loop/` ready for that experiment.

## The Mother Lode v3.1 increment

Mother Lode shipped as v3 in April 2026 with an AUC of 0.85 under
spatial-block CV, then iterated through eight v3.1 experiments that
either improved or tightened the result:

- **B1.** Magnetic-field derivatives (1VD, HGM, analytic signal,
  tilt) added. About 1 pp AUC gain.
- **B2.** SGMC MAJOR1/2/3 fine-grained lithology one-hots
  factorized and encoded. Modest gain, mostly improves SHAP
  feature-attribution interpretability.
- **C1.** Weighted-PU port from Hajihosseinlou et al. 2025 with
  Optuna TPE search on the weight ratio. Implemented and scored
  alongside the bagging-PU baseline.
- **C2.** Geology-only model plus Klamath cross-region transfer.
  Klamath AUC 0.58, barely above random. Documented as a known
  limit, not hidden.
- **D1.** MRDS Cox-Singer-cleaned positive labels dropped 20% of
  the v3 positives that were placer, epithermal, or skarn-Au
  rather than in-place orogenic. AUC roughly unchanged; the cleanup
  is for correctness, not performance.

The C2 experiment is worth pulling out. I trained a Sierra model
on geology only (lithology one-hots, MAJOR1/2/3 fields, age, distance
to fault, basic topography) and dropped the regionally-shifting
features (magnetic, gravity, NGDB geochemistry, Sentinel-2). On
Klamath the geology-only model captures 1.27x more positives in the
top 1% than a random sample would, where the full-feature v3 model
captured only 0.41x as many (worse than random). Past the top 1%
the model falls back below random again. So feature shift in the
geophysical and remote-sensing layers explains some of the transfer
failure, but even with that out of the way the model trained on
Sierra spatial context does not generalize past the very top cells
in Klamath.

The Mother Lode AOI is also covered cell-for-cell by the northern
Sierra placer model in [Chapter 4](placer.qmd). I built the placer
AOI as a superset of the Mother Lode lode-raster extent on purpose,
so the two grids register exactly and goldbug can ingest the same
placer GeoTIFF as both the Mother Lode lode-gold layer and the
Mother Lode placer layer. The Tertiary deep-gravel and Quaternary
modern-channel components are separately calibrated rasters that
get fused per-cell with `np.maximum` into the single deliverable
goldbug consumes. Methods for the placer side are in Chapter 4.

## What the four regions together show

**The methodology reproduces across deposit type and region.** Top-1%
capture of 22% on Tanacross (porphyry-Cu) and 23% on Arizona
(porphyry-Cu, different continental crust setting) lands in the
same regime. The pipeline is portable.

**Cross-region transfer is the unsolved frontier.** Mother Lode →
Klamath (geology-only): AUC 0.58. Eastern Alaska v1 blind test
against Kenorland's 2023 porphyry intersection scored at the 62nd
percentile of the model's prospectivity ranking; the diagnostic
traced this to exploration bias in the training labels. Four
documented remediations did not rescue the blind test. The fix
needs methodology-level changes. See
[Chapter 4 audits](reproductions.qmd) for how the published
literature deals with the same problem (it mostly doesn't).

**SHAP feature attribution matches geology.** On Mother Lode the
top features are distance-to-greenstone-belt, distance-to-fault,
NGDB arsenic and antimony, plus the Calaveras Complex and Mariposa
Slate lithology one-hots, which is the canonical orogenic-Au
recipe. On Arizona the top features are AGDB4 Cu and Mo
stream-sediment, porphyry-host intrusive lithology, and
distance-to-fault, which is the canonical porphyry-Cu recipe.
Reasonable model behavior.

Placer prospectivity is a different problem from the four lode pipelines
in this chapter (two-population fusion, hydraulic-pit positives, isotonic
calibration on a 250 m grid). It now lives in its own chapter; see
[Chapter 4: Placer-Au prospectivity (northern Sierra)](placer.qmd).

## Deeper notebooks

- [Eastern Alaska v1 integrated report](./notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity.html): full pipeline plus Kenorland blind test diagnostic.
- [BC Golden Triangle v2 integrated report](./notebooks/bcgt/bcgt_porphyry_prospectivity.html): 154-positive blind-drill validation against post-2015 holes.
- Mother Lode: [v3.1 improvements (8 experiments)](./notebooks/motherlode/v3p1_improvements.html), [v3 data exploration](./notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration.html).
- [Arizona v4 integrated report](./notebooks/arizona/main.html): Cox-Singer-cleaned 191-positive porphyry-Cu set with 4-cell DEEP-SEAM-style methodology comparison.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
